title: Manual Film Processing Chemistry and Steps
narrationScript: In manual processing you are the machine, and the image is only as repeatable as your habits. Five steps stand between the latent image and a permanent record. Develop, stop, fix, wash, and dry. Every one of them has a time, a temperature, and a reason, and this lesson walks the line tank by tank. First, a word about how we time development. The old way was sight development, pulling the film when it looked right under the safelight. That is guesswork, it ruins consistency, and it has no place in code work. We use the time and temperature method instead. The standard anchor is five minutes in the developer at sixty eight degrees Fahrenheit, and when the bath runs warmer or cooler you read the corrected time from the manufacturer's chart, roughly eight minutes at sixty degrees and about four minutes at seventy two. Below about sixty degrees the chemistry crawls and behaves unpredictably, and above the mid seventies fog rises and the warm emulsion softens, so hold the bath in the working band and keep every other solution within about five degrees of it, because a film moving between baths of very different temperature can shrivel its emulsion into a cracked network called reticulation. The developer itself is a team of chemicals. Reducing agents convert the exposed silver halide grains to black metallic silver, an alkaline activator keeps them energetic, a sulfite preservative slows oxidation from the air, and a bromide restrainer holds back fog by discouraging development of unexposed grains. Your part is agitation and replenishment. When the hanger goes in, tap it sharply to knock off air bubbles, because a bubble clinging to the emulsion blocks development and leaves a clear spot. Then agitate at regular intervals, about every minute, lifting and draining so fresh solution sweeps the surface. Skip it and you get streaks and mottle from exhausted developer dragging across the film. Development consumes chemicals with every film, so add replenisher to hold the level and the activity steady, and when the total replenisher you have added approaches two or three times the tank volume, or when control strips show the bath drifting, dump it and mix fresh. Keep the lid on between runs, because oxidized developer turns brown and weak. And guard the tank jealously, because even a small splash of fixer in the developer poisons it for good. Next comes the stop bath, usually one to three percent acetic acid, for thirty seconds to a minute with constant agitation. It kills development instantly by neutralizing the alkaline developer carried in the emulsion, and it protects the fixer's acidity so the fixer lives longer. A running water rinse can substitute, but it needs more time and more agitation, and it does a poorer job of protecting the fixer. Then the fixer. Its job is to dissolve the unexposed, undeveloped silver halide so the image stops being light sensitive, and its hardener toughens the gelatin for handling and drying. Watch the film clear. The milky look fades as the halide dissolves, and the time that takes is the clearing time. The rule is to fix for at least twice the clearing time, and when the clearing time in a used bath grows to about double what fresh fixer gave, the bath is exhausted, so replenish or replace it. Do not park film in the fixer forever either, because very long fixing slowly bleaches fine image silver. Spent fixer is loaded with silver, and silver recovery is both an environmental requirement and money back. Washing is the step everyone shortchanges, and it is the one your file room will judge you on. Running water, twenty to thirty minutes, with enough flow to exchange the tank volume several times an hour, carries the thiosulfate out of the emulsion. Leave it in and nothing looks wrong today, but over months and years the residue attacks the silver image and the radiograph yellows and fades, which matters because radiographs are retained legal records. A hypo clearing bath after fixing can shorten the wash when water is scarce. Last, the finish. Dip the washed film in a wetting agent for about thirty seconds so water sheets off evenly instead of beading. Skip it and droplets dry into water spots that read like artifacts. Hang films to drain from a corner and dry them in a cabinet with clean filtered air at moderate warmth, films spaced so they never touch. Too much heat makes film brittle, dust in the airflow embeds in soft emulsion, and a drop running down a drying film leaves a permanent streak. One more habit. Respect the chemistry. Wear gloves and eye protection when mixing, always add acid to water, ventilate the room, and keep mercury thermometers out of the darkroom entirely. The process rewards the careful, every single time.
imagePrompt: A technician in deep red safelight lifts a dripping stainless film hanger out of an open developer tank above a row of darkroom processing tanks, faint vapor rising from the warm solutions.

sections.heading: Five Steps and the Time-Temperature Method
sections.body:
Manual processing turns the latent image into a permanent record in five steps - **develop, stop, fix, wash, dry** - plus a wetting-agent rinse that earns its keep at the very end. The developer reduces exposed silver halide grains to black metallic silver; everything after it preserves and protects what the developer built.

Development is timed by the **time-temperature method**, never by watching the film darken under the safelight. Sight development hands every film a different density and contrast and is unacceptable for code work. The anchor is **5 minutes at 68 °F (20 °C)**; off-temperature baths use the corrected time from the manufacturer's chart - longer when cooler, shorter when warmer. Below about 60 °F the reducing agents act too slowly to behave predictably; above the mid-70s fog rises and the warm emulsion softens.

Keep every solution - stop, fixer, wash - within about **5 °F of the developer**. A film moving between baths of very different temperature can suffer **reticulation**: the emulsion swells and shrinks unevenly into a net-like cracked pattern that destroys the radiograph.

sections.caption: The manual processing line in order, with typical times and temperatures for each station from developer to dryer.

sections.heading: Developer and Replenishment
sections.body:
A developer is a team of chemicals: **reducing agents** (classically metol or phenidone teamed with hydroquinone) convert exposed grains to silver; an **alkaline activator** energizes them; a **sulfite preservative** slows aerial oxidation; a **bromide restrainer** suppresses fog by discouraging development of unexposed grains.

Your contribution is agitation and replenishment. Tap the hanger sharply on entry to dislodge **air bells** - bubbles that block developer contact and leave small clear spots. Agitate at regular intervals, about once a minute, lifting the hanger, letting it drain alternately from each corner, and reinserting; without agitation, exhausted developer and reaction products drag across the surface as streaks and mottle. Hold temperature with a water jacket, and keep a **floating lid** on the tank between runs - oxidized developer turns brown and weak.

Every film consumes developer, so add **replenisher** at the manufacturer's rate for the film area processed, holding both solution level and activity steady. When total replenisher added reaches roughly **2-3 times the original tank volume**, or when control films show drift, discard the bath and mix fresh. Guard the tank: even a small splash of fixer permanently poisons a developer, and each tank keeps its own stirring paddle.

sections.caption: Typical time-temperature development chart: 5 minutes at 68 °F, longer when cooler, shorter when warmer, with no development below 60 °F or above about 75 °F.

sections.heading: Stop Bath and Fixer
sections.body:
The **stop bath** - typically **1-3% acetic acid** for **30-60 seconds** with constant agitation - halts development instantly by neutralizing the alkaline developer carried in the emulsion, and it protects the fixer's acidity so the fixer lasts. A plain running-water rinse can substitute (a minute or two with agitation), but it stops development less sharply and shortens fixer life; weak stopping action invites streaking and staining carried into the fixer.

The **fixer** - 'hypo', a sodium or ammonium thiosulfate solution - dissolves the unexposed, undeveloped silver halide so the film stops being light-sensitive, while its **hardener** toughens the gelatin and its acidity arrests any remaining development. Watch the milky cast clear from the film: that interval is the **clearing time**. Fix for **at least twice the clearing time**, and treat a clearing time that has stretched to about **double the fresh-fixer value** as the signal to replenish or replace the bath. Do not over-fix for tens of minutes - prolonged fixing slowly bleaches fine image silver. Spent fixer is heavy with dissolved silver: route it through **silver recovery** - an environmental obligation and a real credit - never down the drain.

sections.heading: Washing, Water Spots, and Drying
sections.body:
**Washing** removes thiosulfate and dissolved silver salts from the emulsion. Use running water with complete exchange - cascade tanks counterflowing toward the fixer end are the classic arrangement - for **20-30 minutes**, with flow giving several complete water changes per hour (4-8 is a common figure), at roughly 60-80 °F. Under-washed film looks perfect today; months or years later the retained hypo attacks the image and the radiograph yellows and fades. Radiographs are retained records - wash for the file room. A **hypo clearing agent** bath after fixing shortens wash times where water is scarce.

Then the **wetting agent**: about **30 seconds** in the dilute surfactant so water sheets off evenly instead of beading. Skipped, droplets dry at different rates than the surrounding emulsion and leave **water spots** that read like indications. Drain films from a corner; if a squeegee is used at all it must be soft, clean, and gentle - grit under a squeegee plows scratches.

**Dry** in a cabinet with clean, **filtered** warm air around 100-120 °F, films spaced so surfaces never touch. Overheating makes film brittle, dust in the airflow embeds in the soft emulsion, and a drop running down a drying film leaves a permanent run mark. Finished films come off the hangers by their edges - they are originals now.

sections.title: Chemical safety in the darkroom
sections.body: Developer is alkaline and a skin sensitizer; stop bath and fixer are acidic. Wear gloves and eye protection when mixing, **always add acid to water**, and keep the ventilation running. Mercury thermometers stay out of the darkroom entirely - one break contaminates the chemistry and the room. Spent fixer is regulated, silver-bearing waste: it goes through recovery, never down the drain.

sections.heading: Reading the Process Like an Inspector
sections.body:
Every processing fault leaves a signature, and a Level II learns to read them backward from the film:

- **Thin, flat films across the board** - weak development: exhausted, diluted, cold, or under-timed developer.
- **Streaks trailing from the hanger clips** - inadequate agitation, or crusted hangers.
- **Small clear round spots** - air bells never tapped loose.
- **Milky or cloudy film** - under-fixed: pulled early, or the fixer is exhausted. Refix promptly.
- **Soft, easily scratched emulsion** - fixer hardener exhausted.
- **Yellow-brown staining in storage** - retained hypo from a short wash.
- **Round light spots** - water droplets dried in place because the wetting agent was skipped.
- **Net-like cracking (reticulation)** - temperature shock between baths.

Diagnose the stage first, then the cause inside that stage - the drill below makes you practice exactly that.

figures.title: Manual processing line: steps, times, temperatures

interactive.title: Processing Fault Diagnosis Drill
interactive.intro: A stack of problem films just came out of your darkroom. For each symptom, decide which stage of manual processing is the prime suspect.

items.label: Every film for two days has come out thin and flat, even with proven exposure techniques
items.why: Uniform low density and low contrast across all films points to weak development - exhausted, diluted, cold, or under-timed developer.

items.label: Small clear round spots, with a faint bubble outline visible at some of them
items.why: Air bells clinging to the emulsion blocked developer contact. Tap the hanger sharply when the film first goes in, then agitate.

items.label: Uneven density streaks trailing down the film from the hanger clips
items.why: Inadequate agitation lets exhausted developer and reaction products drag across the surface - agitate about once a minute, and keep hangers clean.

items.label: Film looks milky and cloudy straight out of the dryer
items.why: Undissolved silver halide remains: the film was pulled before twice its clearing time, or the fixer is exhausted. Refix promptly.

items.label: Today's measured clearing time is double what fresh solution gave
items.why: Doubled clearing time is the standard signal that the fixer is exhausted and must be replenished or replaced.

items.label: Films develop normally but the emulsion comes out soft and scratches at a touch
items.why: The fixer's hardener toughens the gelatin; soft, fragile emulsion means the hardener is exhausted or the fixer was mixed wrong.

items.label: Radiographs that read fine last year now show brownish-yellow staining in the file
items.why: Retained thiosulfate from inadequate washing slowly attacks the silver image - archival staining is a washing failure.

items.label: Round light marks where droplets sat as the film dried
items.why: Water spots: drops dry at a different rate than the surrounding emulsion. A thirty-second wetting-agent dip and corner draining prevent them.

items.label: Films leave the cabinet with embedded dust and run marks down the surface
items.why: Dirty, unfiltered dryer air embeds dust in soft emulsion, and water running from clips leaves permanent run marks - filter the airflow and drain films before they go in.

checkQuestions.question: Your developer is running at 72 °F instead of the standard 68 °F. Using a typical time-temperature chart, the development time should be:
options: 5 minutes - the standard time always applies
About 4 minutes - shorter because the bath is warmer
About 8 minutes - longer to compensate for the heat
About 2 minutes - half time for every 4 degrees
checkQuestions.explanation: Developer activity rises with temperature, so time comes down: a typical chart gives about 4 minutes at 72 °F against the 5-minute, 68 °F anchor. Never guess - read the chart.

checkQuestions.question: The stop bath in a manual line is typically:
options: 1-3% acetic acid for 30-60 seconds with agitation, to halt development and protect the fixer
Concentrated acetic acid for 5 minutes, to harden the emulsion
A sodium thiosulfate solution that dissolves unexposed silver halide
Plain standing water the film rests in until the fixer is free
checkQuestions.explanation: The mild acid neutralizes alkaline developer carried in the emulsion, stopping development at the chosen time and preserving the fixer's acidity. Dissolving halide is the fixer's job.

checkQuestions.question: The standard signal that fixer is exhausted and must be replenished or replaced is:
options: The solution turns dark brown
The clearing time has grown to about double the fresh-fixer value
Films emerge with high density
The bath temperature begins to drop
checkQuestions.explanation: As fixer exhausts, it dissolves halide more slowly, so clearing time stretches; at about twice the fresh value the bath can no longer fix reliably. Brown discoloration is the developer's failure mode.

checkQuestions.question: A radiograph stored for several months develops a yellow-brown stain. The most likely processing cause is:
options: Excessive development time
Inadequate washing that left thiosulfate in the emulsion
Too much wetting agent in the final rinse
A dryer set slightly too warm
checkQuestions.explanation: Retained hypo slowly attacks the silver image, producing the classic delayed yellow-brown archival stain - the reason washing gets 20-30 minutes of running water with real exchange.

checkQuestions.question: The purpose of the wetting-agent dip after washing is to:
options: Neutralize any remaining developer alkalinity
Make water sheet off evenly so droplets cannot dry into water spots
Harden the emulsion before the dryer
Remove dissolved silver from the emulsion
checkQuestions.explanation: About 30 seconds in the dilute surfactant breaks surface tension so the film drains uniformly; without it, droplets dry at different rates and print as spot artifacts.

references: CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Film Quality 2.4-2.4.6 (manual processing of film)
ASTM E999-style guidance on controlling radiographic film processing quality (reference practice)
Film manufacturer time-temperature processing charts (reference practice)
ASME BPVC Section V, Article 2 (radiographic examination, general practice)
